metrics.narrative: Boyd Gaming articulated specific operational pain and quantified ROI projections from Zycus, but the buyer has not yet confirmed their own financial baseline or quantified cost-of-inaction, leaving the value case directional rather than buyer-owned. On the 10 April discovery call, John Meisner (Director of Sourcing) described the pain: 'it takes a while for data to be ingested... we spend time cleaning up data, sorting out, figuring out is this valid information to include in an RFP... for categories not item-driven like IT, facilities, services, professional services, it's being able to quickly pull together all the information to understand what we're doing.' He quantified indirect+direct addressable spend at $700-800 million annually (22 April call). Zycus presented an ROI model projecting $5-12 million in first-year savings based on client data (10 April call), and Meisner's stated goal on the 22 April call was 'if my sourcing specialist can negotiate 3 contracts at a time, can your solution help us negotiate 20 contracts at one time... multiply the amount of work we can get done.' However, Boyd has not shared their own Coupa savings baseline, current manual-effort cost, or quantified the cost of deferring automation (e.g., opportunity cost of tail-spend leakage, procurement team capacity constraint). The buyer requested the ROI model data sheet and a follow-up session to review it (29 April), but that session never occurred, so the quantified value case remains Zycus-presented, not buyer-validated. Status is partial: the business problem and scale are confirmed; the quantified financial impact and cost-of-inaction are not.

champion.narrative: John Meisner (Director of Sourcing, Boyd Gaming) is a developing champion with strong product interest and multi-call engagement, but he has not demonstrated active advocacy (no introductions to Economic Buyer, CFO, or exec sponsor), has not driven internal urgency, and his engagement has gone passive for 58 days since the last call (29 April 2026, no reply to Mario's 17 June outreach). Meisner initiated contact, participated in three calls (10 April discovery, 22 April deeper discovery with agenda, 29 April live demo), brought his sourcing team (Mackenzie Dahn, David) and Coupa system admin (Mike) plus Sarean Sim to the demo, asked detailed pain-based questions, and requested the ROI model and customer benchmarks — all positive signals of interest and internal evangelism. However, he has not moved the deal forward since the demo: he closed the 29 April call saying 'I'll talk internally to see if we want to do a follow-up, maybe do an NDA to get into more details,' but 58 days later no internal decision has been communicated, the NDA has not been executed, and he has not replied to Mario's 17 June re-engagement attempt. He has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer, CFO, VP Finance, or CPO (Eddie Marshall, who is the Boyd Gaming CPO per the account contact list but has never appeared in any activity). He positioned the evaluation as 'research phase' and 'planning for the future' rather than urgent, and he stated 'it's likely not something we're going to do this year' (22 April call), signaling low internal priority. His access to power is unclear: he reports to unnamed 'leadership' who makes budget decisions, but he has not offered to introduce Zycus to that leadership. Champion strength is developing: he is engaged, informed, and aligned on the pain, but he is not actively driving the deal internally, has not secured exec sponsorship, and is not responding to outreach. He is at risk of deprioritization or passive fade. The move is to re-engage Meisner with a time-bound, low-friction next step (e.g., '30-minute ROI model preview — no NDA required — to show you what the savings model looks like for a $700-800M spend base, then you decide if it's worth taking to your CFO'), and if he does not engage within 10 business days, escalate to the deal owner's manager (Alexa Bradley) for a peer-level executive connect to the deal owner's manager or VP Finance to bypass the stall and map power directly.

competition.narrative: Coupa is the incumbent and the primary competitive threat, embedded at Boyd Gaming for 4-5 years and partially deployed for procurement. Meisner stated on the 10 April call that Boyd 'moved to Coupa 4-5 years ago now... going to Coupa is much more efficient than we had before (we had Ariba One before), but the data is similar because of what we put into the system.' On the 29 April demo, the first question from Sarean Sim (Boyd team member) was 'does it integrate with Coupa?' and Zycus confirmed an active Coupa integration exists. The competitive read: Coupa is the incumbent but is not delivering the AI-powered automation Boyd needs — Meisner's pain (manual data cleansing, slow RFP scoping, no autonomous negotiation at scale) is evidence that Coupa's current deployment is functionally incomplete. However, Coupa incumbency creates inertia: Boyd has invested 4-5 years in Coupa, it is 'more efficient than Ariba,' and the buyer has not expressed dissatisfaction strong enough to trigger a rip-and-replace. Zycus is positioned as a complement or enhancement ('we're replacing or enhancing existing investments' per the 22 April call), not a Coupa replacement in year one, which lowers the buyer's perceived risk but also lowers urgency. The wedge: Zycus Merlin AI (agentic intake, autonomous negotiation, AI-powered RFP generation) delivers capabilities Coupa does not have and that Boyd explicitly wants (Meisner's 3-to-20 contracts capacity multiplier goal). Coupa vulnerability: if Coupa cannot demonstrate equivalent AI agentic negotiation and intake orchestration, Zycus wins on innovation. Coupa strength: embedded, lower switching cost if Boyd decides to 'wait and see' if Coupa adds AI later. No other competitors (Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) have been mentioned. The single strongest current threat is Coupa incumbency inertia and the do-nothing / defer-to-2027 scenario, not an active Coupa competitive defense. Mario's 17 June Next Step references 're-validate Meisner's pain points and stance with Coupa,' suggesting Zycus suspects Coupa may be re-engaging or that Meisner is reevaluating Coupa's roadmap. The move is to quantify the cost of staying with Coupa-as-is (tail-spend leakage, manual effort, sourcing capacity constraint) in the ROI model, and to ask Meisner directly: 'Has Coupa shared their AI roadmap with you, and if so, what capabilities are they committing to deliver and on what timeline?' to surface any Coupa countermove and address it head-on.

identify_pain.narrative: Boyd Gaming's operational pain is confirmed and specific, articulated by John Meisner (Director of Sourcing) across the 10 April and 22 April discovery calls, with his sourcing team corroborating the pain through their detailed questions on the 29 April demo. The pain centers on manual, time-intensive data cleansing and RFP scoping that bottlenecks sourcing capacity and prevents the team from scaling to handle larger volumes. On the 10 April call, Meisner described the data-analytics pain: 'it takes a while for data to be ingested... we spend time cleaning up data, sorting out, figuring out is this valid information to include in the RFP process... for categories not item-driven like IT, facilities, services, professional services, it's being able to quickly pull together all the information to understand what we're doing. We have Salesforce agreement with multiple SOWs executed over the past couple years — how do we quickly pull together and say this is our current value of Salesforce, then go into negotiations for the renewal?' He owns the pain. On the 22 April call, he quantified the capacity constraint: 'if my sourcing specialist can negotiate 3 contracts at one time, can your solution help us negotiate 20 contracts at one time... how can we do things quicker, multiply what we're doing, get more work out of resources we have?' The pain is acute for non-catalog, services-heavy categories (IT, facilities, professional services) where Coupa's existing deployment does not provide the AI-powered automation Boyd needs. Meisner also noted on the 10 April call the need for 'deeper insights on supplier market dynamics, pricing trends, timing to inform sourcing strategies' — market intelligence that Coupa is not delivering. The pain owner is Meisner and his sourcing team (Mackenzie Dahn, David), who execute RFPs and contract negotiations manually today. Risk: the pain is real but the urgency is low — Meisner positioned this as 'research phase' and 'planning for the future,' not a burning platform. Coupa has been in place 4-5 years and Boyd is functioning (slowly), so there is no forcing function driving immediate change. The pain is chronic operational inefficiency, not acute business failure, which makes it vulnerable to indefinite deferral. The move is to quantify the cost-of-inaction in the ROI model session: calculate the annual cost of manual RFP prep (sourcing-team hours x burden rate), the tail-spend leakage from contracts Meisner's team cannot negotiate at scale, and the opportunity cost of capacity constraint (e.g., how many more sourcing events could Boyd run per year with 3x-5x automation, and what is the savings impact of that incremental volume).

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec requirements, or SI/partner involvement have been discussed on any call, and Boyd Gaming has not shared their paper process, approval workflow, or contracting timeline. Zycus proposed executing an NDA on the 22 April call ('we always like to do that so we can be fully transparent'), and Meisner agreed in principle ('if and when the time is appropriate'), but the NDA was never executed and is listed as a Zycus commitment from the 29 April call that remains open 58 days later. No MSA, Order Form, SOW, security review, or legal-redline process has been discussed. On the 29 April demo, Sarean Sim (Boyd team member) asked 'what's the level of effort to configure and set it up?' and Zycus replied 'typically a couple months,' but no formal implementation plan, SOW scoping, or procurement-channel requirements (e.g., does Boyd require a reseller, does Workday ERP integration require Workday's approval) have been explored. The Salesforce opportunity shows no tasks or events related to legal, InfoSec, or procurement approvals. Given that Boyd uses Workday ERP (implemented 2 years ago per the 22 April call) and Coupa is embedded, the paper process likely includes: (1) NDA execution (not done); (2) ROI model and business-case approval by finance/CFO (not done); (3) vendor onboarding and security review (not discussed); (4) Workday and Coupa integration validation (mentioned on 29 April demo but not scoped); (5) MSA and Order Form negotiation (not discussed); (6) budget allocation approval (November-December 2026 per Meisner). None of these gates have been addressed, and the deal is stalled before the first gate (NDA). The move is to propose a lightweight NDA + ROI model session as the immediate next step to create forward motion and uncover the downstream paper-process requirements by asking Meisner directly: 'Once we align on the ROI model, what does your internal approval process look like — who needs to sign off, what does legal/InfoSec/procurement need to see, and what is the realistic timeline from business case approval to contract signature?'

decision_process.narrative: Boyd Gaming's decision process is partially understood from Meisner's description of the budget cycle, but the approval chain, selection timeline, and gate criteria remain unconfirmed, and no formal RFP or vendor evaluation process has been announced. Meisner stated on the 22 April call that 'budget process starts September, approvals November-December' for the 2027 budget year, and that 'leadership' (unnamed) makes the decision. He positioned this evaluation as 'research phase... planning for the future' and said 'it's likely not something we're going to do this year.' The call sequence shows: (1) 10 April discovery call (Meisner solo with Zycus); (2) 22 April deeper discovery (Meisner + Zycus, NDA discussed but not executed); (3) 29 April live demo (Meisner + his sourcing managers Mackenzie Dahn and David, plus Mike the Coupa system admin, plus Sarean Sim, role unclear). The progression from solo discovery to team demo suggests Boyd moved from awareness to structured evaluation, but no procurement lead, CFO, or exec sponsor joined, and no RFP timeline, shortlist, or vendor-selection gate was mentioned. Meisner closed the 29 April call saying 'I'll talk internally to see if we want to do a follow-up, maybe do an NDA to get into more details' — indicating the process is still informal and gated by his internal consultation, not a formal procurement calendar. The Salesforce Next Step field (dated 17 June, 9 days ago as of 26 June) shows Mario Castro (deal owner) reaching out to 'get back on track with the possible RFP that they are about to launch,' but no RFP has been confirmed by Boyd, and Meisner has not replied to that outreach (58 days since last buyer engagement). The decision process is therefore: informal research (complete) → internal consultation + NDA (stalled) → ROI model review (not scheduled) → RFP launch (mentioned by Zycus next step but not confirmed by buyer) → budget approval (November-December 2026 for 2027 implementation). The stall point is Meisner's internal consultation, and without a confirmed RFP timeline or exec sponsor engagement, the process is indefinite. The move is to re-engage Meisner, ask directly what internal conversation needs to happen to advance to NDA + ROI model session, and request a 30-day decision checkpoint (e.g., by end of July 2026) to determine if Boyd will proceed to formal evaluation or defer to 2027.

Meisner 22 April call: sourcing capacity multiplier, integration with Workday/Coupa/Cobblestone, risk management lower priority

No formal RFP, BRD, or weighted scorecard provided by Boyd
decision_criteria.narrative: Boyd Gaming's evaluation criteria are partially documented from the three calls but have not been formalized in an RFP, BRD, or written scorecard, and the relative weighting and must-haves versus nice-to-haves remain unclear. Meisner articulated criteria across the discovery and demo sessions: (1) AI-powered procurement automation to accelerate RFP scoping and data analytics (10 April: 'quickly pull together information, determine what's valid, what's not valid to include in the RFP'); (2) autonomous negotiation at scale to multiply sourcing team capacity (22 April: 'can your solution help us negotiate 20 contracts at one time'); (3) seamless integration with existing ERP and procurement tools — Workday (ERP, implemented 2 years ago), Coupa (partially deployed for 4-5 years), and Cobblestone (CLM) — rather than replacing them immediately (22 April: 'preference for solutions that complement existing tools rather than replacing them'); (4) high user adoption via native integration with communication platforms (Microsoft Teams mentioned, 22 April); (5) quantified ROI and cost savings with a credible, data-backed business case (29 April: Sarean asked 'is there something that quantifies effectiveness... actual output'); (6) supplier discovery and risk management (Meisner noted on 22 April that risk management is 'less important than in manufacturing' due to backup suppliers, so this is lower-weighted). However, Boyd has not provided a formal RFP, BRD, or weighted scorecard, and Meisner has not confirmed must-have thresholds (e.g., minimum savings target, max implementation timeline, required Coupa integration depth). The 29 April demo covered intake orchestration, autonomous negotiation (Anna agent), and integration capabilities, and Meisner's team asked detailed questions (Sarean on Coupa integration, configuration effort, customer count, savings proxy), suggesting active evaluation, but the criteria remain conversational and buyer-stated rather than formalized. The next step is to request Boyd's written evaluation criteria or co-create a scorecard during the ROI model session to make the criteria explicit and weighted.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Boyd Gaming exhibits strong AI appetite based on Meisner's articulated pain (manual, time-intensive RFP scoping and data cleansing bottlenecking sourcing capacity), his explicit 3-to-20 contracts capacity multiplier goal, his team's detailed questions on the 29 April demo (Coupa integration, ROI quantification, configuration effort, customer count, savings proxy), and his request for the ROI model and customer benchmarks. The buyer is AI Curious leaning toward AI Hungry: they understand the operational pain AI can solve, they engaged deeply on the Merlin AI demo, and they are actively researching AI-powered procurement automation. However, the urgency is low — Meisner positioned this as 'research phase' and 'planning for the future' rather than a burning platform — and the buyer has not yet committed to a formal evaluation timeline, NDA, or ROI model session, suggesting curiosity has not yet converted to active buying intent. best_practice_check.summary: This deal is pre-budget-cycle due diligence with a passive Director-level champion, no Economic Buyer mapped, and 58 days of silence since the last buyer call. The close date (18 September 2026) conflicts with the buyer's stated 2027 budget timeline (approvals November-December 2026). Coupa is embedded and the evaluation is positioned as research, not urgent replacement, creating high fade risk. The three highest-impact levers to win are: (1) re-engage Meisner with a time-bound, low-friction ROI model preview (no NDA required) to restart momentum, and if no reply in 10 business days, escalate to the deal owner's manager for a peer executive connect to Boyd's CPO or VP Finance to map the Economic Buyer and validate the business case at the exec level; (2) quantify the cost-of-inaction in the ROI model (manual RFP prep cost, tail-spend leakage, sourcing capacity constraint, opportunity cost of not scaling to 20 concurrent negotiations) to make staying with Coupa-as-is the expensive choice and create urgency; (3) surface Coupa's AI roadmap by asking Meisner directly what capabilities Coupa is committing to deliver and on what timeline, then address any Coupa countermove head-on with live customer proof points of Zycus AI in production today.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the sole named competitor and the incumbent, embedded at Boyd Gaming for 4-5 years and partially deployed for procurement. Coupa's current deployment is not delivering the AI-powered automation Boyd needs (Meisner's pain: manual data cleansing, slow RFP scoping, no autonomous negotiation at scale), creating an opening for Zycus Merlin AI (agentic intake, autonomous negotiation, AI-powered RFP generation). However, Coupa incumbency creates inertia: Boyd has invested years in Coupa, it is 'more efficient than Ariba,' and Meisner has not expressed dissatisfaction strong enough to trigger a rip-and-replace. Zycus is positioned as a complement or enhancement, not a year-one replacement, which lowers buyer risk but also lowers urgency. The single strongest current threat is Coupa incumbency inertia and the do-nothing / defer-to-2027 scenario, not an active Coupa competitive defense. Mario's 17 June Next Step suggests Zycus suspects Coupa may be re-engaging or Meisner is reevaluating Coupa's roadmap. No other competitors (Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) have been mentioned. Wedge: quantify the cost of staying with Coupa-as-is (tail-spend leakage, manual effort, sourcing capacity constraint) and surface Coupa's AI roadmap to address any countermove head-on.

ai_positioning_strength.score: Strong product-market fit but incomplete business-case delivery
ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus Merlin AI (agentic intake orchestration, autonomous negotiation agent Anna, AI-powered RFP generation) is well-positioned as the wedge against Coupa incumbency and directly aligned with Boyd Gaming's stated pain (manual data cleansing, slow RFP scoping, sourcing capacity constraint) and Meisner's explicit goal to multiply sourcing team capacity from 3 concurrent contracts to 20. The 29 April demo showcased Merlin Intake (conversational AI via Teams), Anna agent (autonomous tail-spend negotiation), and AI-driven RFP/questionnaire generation using historical data, and Boyd's team (Sarean Sim, Mackenzie, David, Mike) asked detailed, engaged questions on Coupa integration, ROI quantification, configuration effort, customer count, and savings effectiveness, all signals of serious AI appetite. However, Zycus has not yet delivered the ROI model, customer count, or savings benchmarks that Boyd explicitly requested, and the AI value case remains Zycus-presented rather than buyer-validated. The deal is under-positioned because the Merlin AI narrative has not been converted into a quantified, buyer-owned business case that Boyd can champion internally to secure 2027 budget. Score: strong product-market fit and demo engagement, but incomplete business-case delivery. Not under-positioned on capability; under-positioned on buyer ownership and internal championing.
